embeddings.content:
# Retrieval-Augmented Generation Overview

## What RAG Means

Retrieval-augmented generation, or RAG, is a system pattern that combines
document retrieval with language model generation. Instead of asking a model
to answer only from its training data, a RAG system retrieves relevant
documents or chunks first and then asks the model to answer with that context.
This design reduces hallucination and improves factual grounding.

## Core Pipeline

A typical RAG pipeline has four main stages. The first stage is document
ingestion, where files are uploaded, parsed, normalized, and chunked. The
second stage is indexing, where each chunk is converted into an embedding and
stored in a vector index. The third stage is retrieval, where a user query is
embedded and compared against stored vectors to find the most relevant chunks.
The final stage is answer generation, where a language model uses the retrieved
chunks as context to produce a response.

## Why Chunking Matters

Chunking is one of the most important design choices in a RAG system. If chunks
are too small, important context may be split apart and retrieval quality will
drop. If chunks are too large, embeddings may become less specific and relevant
details can be diluted. Many systems start with fixed-size character chunking
and later move toward paragraph-aware or heading-aware chunking.

## Role of Embeddings

Embeddings convert text into dense vectors that capture semantic meaning.
Similar texts should have nearby vectors in embedding space. In a RAG system,
both document chunks and user queries are embedded, and similarity search is
used to rank candidate chunks. Better embedding models often improve retrieval,
but they do not solve every problem. Poor chunking or noisy documents can still
produce weak search results.

## Retrieval and Reranking

The first retrieval stage usually returns a set of candidate chunks with vector
similarity scores. This stage is sometimes called recall-oriented retrieval.
Many production systems then apply a reranker to reorder the candidates based
on stronger query-document matching. Reranking is useful when relevant chunks
are present in the candidate set but are not ranked highly enough by the
embedding search step.

## Agent Use Cases

An enterprise agent system can use RAG as a knowledge layer. The agent first
retrieves relevant documents, then decides whether it should answer directly or
call a tool. For example, the agent may retrieve architecture documentation and
then decide to create a ticket, query a database, or summarize implementation
steps for an engineer.

## Evaluation and Observability

A strong RAG system needs evaluation and observability. Engineers should track
retrieval latency, answer latency, embedding provider, chat provider, and the
top retrieved chunks for each query. They should also evaluate whether relevant
chunks are retrieved at all, whether the model uses the retrieved context
faithfully, and whether fallback paths are triggered during provider failures.

## Common Failure Modes

Common RAG failure modes include poor chunk boundaries, low-quality source
documents, weak embeddings, and missing reranking. Another failure mode occurs
when the user asks a question that is not actually covered by the source
documents. In that case, a grounded system should say that the context is
insufficient instead of inventing an answer.
